While giving it a second look, I noticed that your description for `_dict` is incorrect. `_dict` specifies the class of the returned toml dictionary. The default is the default python `dict`.

One example use case would be to pass `toml.loads(s, _dict=OrderedDict) in order to get a consistent ordering when converting from a toml file to a dictionary and then back out to a toml file.

Otherwise, it looks good (grammar, etc.)

If you build the mappy extension from a github clone, you need to have [cython](http://cython.org) installed. Cython generates mappy.c from mappy.pyx and cmappy.pxd. We don't commit this file because it is derived.

The tar-ball from the [mappy Pypi page](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/mappy) comes with mappy.c generated by cython. This allows you to run `pip install mappy` without cython. You can download that tar-ball and run `python setup.py build_ext`.

> Can you briefly tell me about this replen as it is the only parameter that is not stored inside the mm_reg1_t?

*replen* is calculated by *collect_matches*() in map.c. It is the sum of length of regions covered by highly repetitive k-mers. You can't infer *replen* from a list of *mm_reg1_t*, unfortunately.

> or should I dump all the replen values for all mappings and then add them back?

You have to save *replen* for each read in each batch. You don't need to save it for each *mm_reg1_t* (that's why *replen* is not a member of *mm_reg1_t*). When you generate the final MAPQ, you read all *replen* and choose the largest (or average; not sure) *replen* as the value fed to *mm_set_mapq*().

Note that it is not possible to generate identical alignment for each read because minimap2 is throwing away k-mers with high occurrences and this set of k-mers differs between a uni-part index and a multi-part index. Nonetheless, this only affects a tiny fraction of reads and these reads tend to have low MAPQ. The impact to downstream processing should be little.

Currently this is not that easy, because the versions are automatically determined by[ `pbr` ](https://docs.openstack.org/pbr/3.1.1/#version)when a commit is tagged. 

A posible solution is to use `pbr` to generate `symfit.__version__` as is done [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/40562270/how-to-load-package-version-into-version-variable-if-you-are-using-pbr), but that introduces `pbr` as a dependency to `symfit`, which I think is weird since it is a packaging tool which has nothing to do with fitting.

As for further documentation, a quick description of it's usage on [this page](http://symfit.readthedocs.io/en/latest/fitting_types.html) is in order for such a shinny feature. That way this feature is better advertised, documented, and googleable.

@jamestwebber - This is released in v2.1.0. The calling convention is the same as in `aegea batch`: `aegea launch  akislyuk-ebs-test4 --storage /foo=500 /bar=600`. Caveats:

- The volume type is hardcoded to be st1
- The filesystem type is hardcoded to be ext4
- Mountpoints must exist for mounts to succeed (although you can log in, mkdir the mounts (`mkdir /foo /bar` for the above example), and run `mount -a`, and the rest of it will work just fine).
- If you want the instance to automatically manage the filesystems for you, *you must rebuild the aegea base AMI* using `aegea build_ami NAME` for this to work. The change that I committed today reconfigures the base AMI to support auto-formatting, auto-mounting, and fstab enrollment of the filesystems. (You can log in to the instance and format and mount the filesystem manually using old/non-aegea AMIs.)

@jayvdb Just in case you find it useful, you should be aware that green always install three binaries:

    green
    greenX
    greenX.Y

When green is installed in multiple system python versions, `green` gets overwritten (last one installed wins).

When green is installed in multiple system python versions of the same major version X, then `greenX` gets overwritten.

If you install green in multiple system python versions, which each have a distinct major/minor combo, then you have the option of using the `greenX.Y` commands, since they won't have been overwritten.  For example, you could install green under Python 2.7.9, 3.4.2, and 3.5.0, and use `green2.7`, `green3.4`, and `green3.5`.

Thanks for raising this!

At the moment, you cannot add layers after the figure has been rendered (sorry -- that could have been documented better). The ability to add / remove layers after the map has been rendered would be great.

One possible workaround is to embed the map in a container widget (like an `HBox`) and just rerender a new map inside the `HBox`:

```py

import ipywidgets as widgets

import gmaps
gmaps.configure(api_key='stuff')

class UpdatingMap(object):
    def __init__(self):
        self.layers = []
        self.map_kwargs = {
            'layout': {'width': '600px', 'height': '400px'}
        }
        self.container = widgets.HBox(
            [gmaps.Map(layers=self.layers, **self.map_kwargs)], 
        )
        
    def add_layer(self, layer):
        self.layers.append(layer)
        self.container.children = [
            gmaps.Map(layers=self.layers, **self.map_kwargs)]
        
    def display(self):
        return self.container
```

You can then use:

```py
fig = UpdatingMap()
fig.display()
```

If you now write:

```py
fig.add_layer(gmaps.traffic_layer())
```

... the map updates (or rather, it's replaced by an entirely new map).

Obviously, you need to migrate any state that you want to keep from the old map to the new map. At the moment, this is possible for some things but not others. For instance, there is no way to get the current zoom and center of a map.

Anyone interested in implementing this correctly needs to:

 - in the `PlainmapView` class (https://github.com/pbugnion/gmaps/blob/master/js/src/Map.js#L45), add a listener for a change in the `layers` traitlet in the model. When this changes, the view list `layerViews` needs to be re-updated from the model.
 - At the moment, the view list does not know how to remove layers. For this to work, we need to add a `removeFromMapView` for every layer. In most cases, this probably just amounts to setting `this.map` to `null`.

@dtantsur Nice to meet you Dmitry. Thanks for explaining your context.

Here are the issues with TextFSM. There are three versions of it (gtextfsm, jtextfsm, and textfsm). I believe gtextfsm is dead and no longer maintained; jtextfsm doesn't have the clitable component which I need.

So that only leaves `textfsm` which is maintained by Google. Unfortunately, they used dependencies that were Linux only dependencies. Even though in the Netmiko use case, I don't need any of these Linux-TextFSM specific things.

The consequence of this was that loading textfsm breaks Netmiko on Windows (i.e. it would cause just an import of netmiko to break on Windows).

This really only happens in the `clitable` part of TextFSM and not in the `textfsm` part. Consequently, I wanted to be able to get updates and bugfixes for textfsm without having to maintain my own version of it.

The `clitable` part is a pretty simple function and I doubt other changes to `textfsm` will break it. 

So that is why I put only this `clitable` part internal to Netmiko. 

I am open to other solutions though.

From a practical standpoint, you can stay on Netmiko 2.0.0. That 2.0.1 fix was almost entirely to fix that Windows issue.

This will give us more time to resolve the issue (i.e. we can figure out a solution that works for the next Netmiko release).

```
Logger.root = root
```

When the name is defined, Jedi only evaluates the first branch. This happens to improve Jedi's type inference precision. It's not a performance optimization.

2.These little conversions are necessary to correctly interpret the YUV byte string. If you specify a certain resolution, say 100x100, the actual pixel count will be slighty different. The horizontal resolution is rounded up to the nearest multiple of 32 pixels, while the vertical resolution is rounded up to the nearest multiple of 16 pixels. Thats how the camera chip stores the information. for 100x100 specified, the actual size of `Y` would be 128x112, but since you specified the resolution at camera.resolution, a few overhanging pixels will be uninitialized.

The namedtuple classes are not created directly using `collections.namedtuple()` because, with so many object types, that would result in a lot of duplicate code. `collections.namedtuple()` is still used under the hood, but it is wrapped in the `_create_class()` function, whose jobs include (but not limited to):

- translate `from` field to `from_` field
- give a default value of `None` to fields that have no default value specified
- recursively convert dictionary into namedtuple object for non-simple fields. For example, to place a `User` object or `Chat` object where appropriate. This recursive conversion is only done for *incoming* dictionary (e.g. `Message`). For *outgoing* objects (e.g. `InlineQueryResultArticle`), you are supposed to fill in the appropriate objects yourself.

Ideally, if you want to create an empty `Message()`, it *should* work because all absent fields should be defaulted to `None`. It is not *meant* to be created this way, but it should work. Just like a `Video` object (another incoming object), you get no error doing it this way:
```python
>>> from telepot.namedtuple import Video
>>> v = Video()
>>> v
Video(file_id=None, width=None, height=None, duration=None, thumb=None, mime_type=None, file_size=None, file_path=None)
```

Unfortunately for `Message`, the `from_` field complicates matters. In module `telepot.namedtuple`, I use this line to create a mapping between `from_` and `from`:
```python
keymap = [(k.rstrip('_'), k) for k in filter(lambda e: e in ['from_'], field_names)]
```
then do the replacement in the passed-in keyword arguments:
```python
for oldkey, newkey in keymap:
    kwargs[newkey] = kwargs[oldkey]
    del kwargs[oldkey]
```
A `KeyError` thus occurs if the passed-in keyword arguments have no `from`, *before* the default value can take effect. 

In real life, if `Message` is used according to intention, the `KeyError` would never occur because the `from` field always exists in a message. 

So, this may be called a bug, or it may not, depending on how robust you want telepot to be. (I am inclined to say "yes, it's a bug, because the supposed default value is not taking effect.")

To fix the root of the problem, I think this would do:
```python
for oldkey, newkey in keymap:
    if oldkey in kwargs:
        kwargs[newkey] = kwargs[oldkey]
        del kwargs[oldkey]
```
After this change. `Message()` should work. But `Message(from_=...)` still would not, because it always expects the untampered `from` in the original keyword arguments.

I'm not super familiar with how v0 of ListOffsetRequest was handled, but the [KIP-79](https://cwiki.apache.org/confluence/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=65868090) states that:
```
In ListOffsetRequest/ListOffsetResponse v0, we return a list of offsets which is smaller
than or equals to the target time. This was because the timestamp search is based on
the segment create time. In order to make sure not to miss any messages, users may
have to consume from some earlier segments. After KIP-33, we can accurately find the
messages based on the timestamps, so there is no need to return a list of offsets to the
users anymore.
```
So the search on Kafka 0.10.0 will be quite inaccurate, do you think that's acceptable?

One reason to do this is to increase chances for parallelism, which means minimizing chances for GIL contention.  The following concepts modify simulation behavior and may be implemented in Python:

* Custom fitness models.
* Recorders
* Population size changes for single-deme simulations

We need to find a means of minimizing requests for GIL acquisition.  I think this is easy for the population size changes, as we should be able to access the buffer directly via a pointer (PyArray_DATA, https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy-1.13.0/reference/c-api.array.html).

For recorders, there is probably no choice but to acquire the GIL.

For custom fitness models, it would be nice to have a run-time method of determining if they are Python-implemented vs C++ implemented.  We will have to experiment elsewhere to see what is possible.

> Running coverage twice in a row would give two distinct data files, then implicit combining would combine them together? That seems wrong.

It's quite possible that my perspective is badly skewed by the fact that this is already the world I live in.  I don't have *any* projects with only a single test environment, and once you have 2, you want to be able to speed up runs with `detox`, so parallel coverage is *de rigeur* even if your test suite itself has zero parallelism.

But let me take it as a given that `coverage run thing1; coverage run thing2; coverage report` reporting only on `thing2` is desirable behavior.  How do you get this with parallel coverage too?  One strategy that occurs to me is that we add a discrete notion of a "coverage group", and give it its own identity.  A coverage group would be a UUID with an optional descriptive label, and a timestamp.  (I confess that "group" might be a terrible name for this concept, but, just to have something to call it...)

```console
$ coverage group new --label "lol"
2e843587-a60c-4fab-bfaf-dab910437bb0
$ coverage run --group 2e843587-a60c-4fab-bfaf-dab910437bb0
$ coverage report --group 2e843587-a60c-4fab-bfaf-dab910437bb0
report for 'lol' ...
```

Without `--group`, `coverage run` implicitly creates a new group, and `coverage report` reports on the group with the latest creation timestamp.

You could then have `coverage group --list` to give you a nicely presented list of timestamps, IDs, and labels.  (Groups would presumably be files with a naming convention, like `.covgroup.$UUID` with the timstamp and label in a very simple format inside them.)

To run a test suite that might run some subprocesses, you could then do, say:

```console
$ coverage spawn detox
| / - \ | etc
```
Like `run`, this implicitly makes a new group, then runs `detox` as a subprocess with the coverage group ID as part of its environment.
```
$ coverage report
```
This reports on the group created by `spawn`, since it is the group with the most recent timestamp in the current directory.

Another advantage of this approach is that you could run multiple tests and report on their combined coverage at the same time in the same project, and you wouldn't need any special locking; `.coverage` files could be named `.coverage.$GROUP.$HOST.$PID` and commands like `report` could combine just the relevant files.

After A LOT of search I discovered some strange problem that Mac OS has with TK. Basically, the build that comes embedded with MAC OS of TCL/TK has a bug since 10.6 that don't play nice with some characters.

Python website recommend that you replace the original TCL/TK on MAC OS with another one that don't have the bug. After that, Python will automatically forward all TCL/TK use (easygui included) to the custom version you installed instead of the native pre build version o MAC OS.

Install this file: ActiveTcl8.5.18.0.298892-macosx10.5-i386-x86_64-threaded.dmg and you should stop receiving this problems. The link is below:

http://downloads.activestate.com/ActiveTcl/releases/8.5.18.0/

```python
@property
def category_axis(self):
    """

    category axes are defined.
```

    # load page

There is `https://github.com/jmcarp/flask-apispec/blob/master/tasks.py` file in which there is an install section where it goes to npm to get the latest swagger-ui but it does not end up being in the python package nor is it user replaceable because the static folder is being looked for in the site-packages/flask-apispec...etc path in which the user should not manually modify anything. I basically changed this dynamic approach and statically moved in the swagger ui, not to mention that the templating requires all the `src` attributes to be prefixed with the flask static file template handles so that it can work reliably. 

Also the whole APISPEC_SWAGGER_URL and APISPEC_SWAGGER_UI_URL distinction is calling for trouble. assuming you have a reverse proxy that maps your endopoints to some prefix or does rewrites... I am not sure if swagger ui would be able to find swagger in every possible case. Because unfortunately the javascript on the client side needs to initiate the query with the full URL otherwise it would not load. However if swagger-ui and swagger are always next to each other the logic is simple to generate the correct url and that way the config does not have to contain the full SERVER_URL, which improves portability and flexibility.

Regarding the addition of an integer field to the Tx data structure: I do not think that it would significantly impact performance, given that this data structure already contains arrays. 

To avoid this, we could return the transaction size in deserializer.read_tx, but that would affect more lines of code than the current proposal. 

Finally, completely isolating the size computation in mempool.py would probably require to parse every transaction twice, and that would be far worse for performance than the addition of a simple field in Tx.

Let say I collected some .pgn from different sources to produce a big one. It can happen that some of them contains games with some missing tags from seven tag roster. There is no problem here, PGN import format spec enables this and chess.pgn.read_pgn() can read them. Quoting http://json.org/  :
 `An object is an unordered set of name/value pairs. An object begins with { (left brace) and ends with } (right brace). Each name is followed by : (colon) and the name/value pairs are separated by , (comma).`

So you basically can't lean on the sequence of attributes in a json document; you can only sort lists. 

Compared this with: 
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/10844064/items-in-json-object-are-out-of-order-using-json-dumps
it seems that python supports sorting objects if you explicitely make it to.

You usually should lean on access by the key to object attributes, not by their sequence.

Hey guys been working on a project where I have to extract all the text as well a as images from a .docx file however python-docx only seems to extract the text. I managed to achieve the desired effect by combining python-docx with docx2txt. I essentially create a temp folder extract the images with docxtotxt process them using textract via python-docx and then deleting the temp folder. My code is posted underneath should anyone require this functionality:

```
def parse_docx_file(file):
    text = textract.process(file)
    if not os.path.exists(temp_images):
        os.mkdir(temp_images)
    docx2txt.process(file, temp_images)
    if os.listdir(temp_images):
        files = glob.glob(temp_images + "/*")
        for file in files:
            text += textract.process(file)
    shutil.rmtree(temp_images)
    print(text.decode())
```
Final point the code is written in Python 3.4

Oh, incidentally, we aren't strictly building the zipfile in RAM. We're using tempfile.SpooledTemporaryFile, which uses RAM until the data gets too big, then moves it to a tempfile. If you're seeing a MemoryError, that implies that either the SpooledTemporaryFile is not correctly moving to disk when it should, or that your `/tmp` directory (or `$TMPDIR` or something) is on a ramdisk, so it can't overflow when it needs to. (do you see out-of-memory errors in your kernel logs? or if you watch the system with 'top', does it show the memory usage of `wormhole send` growing unreasonably?)

Now, the best solution is still to stream the directory out, as described in #58, because that wouldn't need a 24GB tempfile. But you might be able to make this work by changing `$TMPDIR` to point at a real disk, instead of a ramdisk.

Or, if you discover that SpooledTemporaryFile isn't doing the switchover thing correctly, maybe there's another bug lurking in here.

The problem (as far as I could discern) when using a masked array occurs when all elements in a single row (or column, depending on the axis) are NaNs. In this case a bug occurs because `scipy.stats.rankdata` tries to index an empty array. If not for this SciPy bug the original code should work, and the bug only occurs in this specific case when an entire row contains only NaN elements.

In my fix I don't mask the NaN elements in the array anymore, so `rankdata` will assign the highest ranks to NaN elements. Then I simply refer back to the original array (notice that line 494 checks for NaN's in `X`, not in `ranks`) and set the rank of the NaN elements to NaN to produce the same behavior as documented for [`bottleneck.nanrankdata`](http://berkeleyanalytics.com/bottleneck/reference.html#bottleneck.nanrankdata).

According to the FSF, anything that important (or statically links) is considered a derived work under the GPL and therefore must also be GPL if distributed.https://softwareengineering.stackexchange.com/questions/87446/using-a-gplv3-python-module-will-my-entire-project-have-to-be-gplv3-licensedThanks anyway.On May 27, 2017 09:45, ingolemo <notifications@github.com> wrote:I do not think that simply typing import lenses requires your project to change license.
In my opinion, you can license your own project under MIT and make use of lenses just fine. The GPL only begins acting when the user installs lenses alongside your code (with pip install your_project, for example), but at that point it is out of your hands and the responsibility of the user.
The GPL only affects your project if your project is actively distributing code from lenses. For example, if you distributed your code together with lenses in some kind of compiled bundle such as you would get with pyinstaller, py2exe, or similar tools. The bundle would fall under GPL and you would need to provide the source code for everything in it, but since you're distributing under MIT you are already doing that.
It is not my intention to force you to use any particular foss license for your own code. I merely want to discourage lenses' use in proprietary software. LGPL is insufficient.

    if inline:

    else:
        return fig.axes

If `inline` is left alone `missingno` will draw the plot and no more.

After some thought about the syntax, I've changed it to be more flexible. A definition of a figure, table, equation etc is ``` [#CounterId optional pre_text @refid optional_pos_text] ``` If `CounterId` isn't defined then `refid` is set to be 1. Otherwise it is the next value of `CounterId`. The HTML which is output is ```html ``` So, for example ``` [#tablecounter Table @mytable: some interesting stuff] ``` will output ```html ``` assuming that this is the first reference to `tablecounter` in the document. When you wish to refer to the table you use a link of the form ``` [@refid] ``` which outputs the html ```html counter_val ``` So in the example above, markdown like this ``` In Table [@mytable] we see something interesting ``` will output ```html In Table 1 we see something interesting ``` Different counters can have different styles by using CSS selectors ```css a.tablecounter: {color: green} figcaption.tablecounter: {font-size: smaller} ``` for example.

Mammoth is designed to convert semantically marked up documents into sensible HTML, rather than performing a high-fidelity conversion to represent the original document as closely as possible. For instance, in general, preserving page numbering doesn't make sense in an HTML document, nor is it clear how a footer should be handled.

If you have specific suggestions on how things like footers should be handled, then I'd be happy to hear them, although I might not have much time to work on it.

If you are looking to produce HTML that resembles the original as closely as possible, I'd suggest looking for an alternative project since this is a use-case that Mammoth intentionally does not handle. If you just want to display the Word document in a web page, have you considered using [Microsoft's online Office document viewer](https://products.office.com/en-us/office-online/view-office-documents-online)?

I found the root cause : 
Actually, "style2" is a Paragraph Style name, RichText uses Charater Style name.
So when doing RichText(...,style="style2") do ... nothing !
It works in the loop because you set the style2/style3 as paragraph style (upper and lower lines are paragraph, styles are duplicated by the loop itself)
For text8, if you look carefully in the docx : there is no paragraph style, but a Caracter Style ("Style2 Char") : that is the reason why it appears bold in the template but not in the generated doc.

So the solution is :  Create you own **CHARACTER** STYLE (without space in the name for compatibility reason)
It is important to create your own style because predefined ones may have other internal name.

_From bower.json specification_

### main

*Recommended*

Type: `String` or `Array` of `String`

The entry-point files necessary to use your package. Only one file per filetype.

Entry-point files have module exports and may use module imports. While Bower does not directly use `main` files, they are listed with the commands `bower list --json` and `bower list --paths`, so they can be used by build tools.

Let's say your package looks like this:

    package
      js/
        motion.js
        run.js
        walk.js
      sass/
        motion.scss
        run.scss
        walk.scss
      img/
        motion.png
        walk.png
        run.png
      fonts/
        icons.woff2
        icons.woff
      dist/
        movement.js
        movement.min.js
        movement.css
        movement.min.css

`motion.js` has module imports for `run.js` and `walk.js`. `motion.scss` has module imports for `run.scss` and `walk.scss`. `main` would be

```
"main": [
  "js/motion.js",
  "sass/motion.scss",
]
```

Image and font files may be used or referenced within the JS or Sass files, but are not `main` files as they are not entry-points.

* Use source files with module exports and imports over pre-built distribution files.
* Do not include minified files.
* Do not include assets files like images, fonts, audio, or video
* Filenames should not be versioned (Bad: `package.1.1.0.js`; Good: `package.js`).
* Globs like `js/*.js` are not allowed.

OK, after testing with stresstest.py, I have found a cause of the problem and way to fix it. By the way, the similar way I have fixed the ConcurrentRotatingFileHandler module last year...

Sometimes, calling lock(self.stream_lock, LOCK_EX) could cause Resource deadlock avoided exception from portalocker. Not sure why does it happen more frequently with Python 3.5. I was not able to catch the same problem with Python 2.7. Most likely it relates to the different versions of the Microsoft DLLs used.

You emit() code catches this exception, skip writing, but tries to unlock file:

            finally:
                self._do_unlock()

Because it is not locked - process crashes there.

Initially I used the self.islocked property to track that. That helped to avoid crashes. But having some logs missing because of the deadlock exceptions is not good. So here is my solution:

`...`
`#from concurrent_log_handler.portalocker import LOCK_EX, LOCK_NB, LockException, lock, unlock`
`from portalocker import LOCK_EX, LOCK_NB, LockException, lock, unlock`
`...`

    def __init__(self, filename, mode='a', maxBytes=0, backupCount=0,
                 encoding=None, debug=False, delay=0, use_gzip=False,
                 owner=None, chmod=None, umask=None):
        ...
        self.islocked = False

    def _do_lock(self):
        self._open_lockfile()
        if self.stream_lock:
            lock(self.stream_lock, LOCK_EX)
            for i in range(10):
                try:
                    lock(self.stream_lock, LOCK_EX)
                    self.islocked = True
                    break
                except:
                    continue
            else:
                raise RuntimeError("Cannot acquire lock after 10 attempts")
       else:
            self._console_log("No self.stream_lock to lock", stack=True)
 
    def _do_unlock(self):
        if self.stream_lock:
            if self.islocked:
                unlock(self.stream_lock)
                self.islocked = False
            self.stream_lock.close()
            self.stream_lock = None
        else:
            self._console_log("No self.stream_lock to unlock", stack=True)

I believe the core difference is the https://github.com/Yelp/py_zipkin/blob/master/py_zipkin/thrift/__init__.py#L181

My issue was caused by using old http transport code, that basically was:

```
def http_transport(encoded_span):
    # copypasted from: https://github.com/Yelp/py_zipkin#transport
    # The collector expects a thrift-encoded list of spans. Instead of
    # decoding and re-encoding the already thrift-encoded message, we can just
    # add header bytes that specify that what follows is a list of length 1.
    body = b'\x0c\x00\x00\x00\x01' + encoded_span
    requests.post(
        'http://{}/api/v1/spans'.format(ZIPKIN),
        data=body,
        headers={'Content-Type': 'application/x-thrift'},
    )
```

As I understand: b'\x0c\x00\x00\x00\x01' - stands for list with length 1 and 0.9.0 already encodes everything as a list so it doesn't required anymore.

@babu-upcomer this issue will probably require a refactoring in our docs because it's not clear how our instrumentation works. You have many ways to instrument your application:
1. using the `ddtrace-run` script, that will instrument all supported libraries automatically, without changing your code. Unfortunately, this is not entirely true, because some integrations aren't enabled out of the box (i.e. `requests`). Anyway, the promise is that it instruments all supported libraries, so it's something we're going to change next. To enable/disable a specific integration, you can do the following (it's an example):
```
$ DATADOG_PATCH_MODULES=requests:true,celery:false ddtrace-run uwsgi [...]
```
2. using manual instrumentation where you pick from our doc the integration you want to enable, and then you follow the steps to enable it. For instance, in Django you need to add our Django app to enable Django instrumentation. Doing that doesn't mean that you have enabled all other integrations. It happens only for Django
3. using manual instrumentation, but activating some specific integrations using our patching API. It means you can do:
```python
from ddtrace import patch

patch(requests=True)
```
To answer your question, I think you can use one of the following approaches:
* you use the `ddtrace-run` wrapper in all your scripts so that you don't change your code. In that case you should set the `DATADOG_PATCH_MODULES=requests:true` env var
* you use the `ddtrace-run` script and in your Django settings you add the snippet defined in step (3) with the `patch(requests=True)`
* you ignore the `ddtrace-run` script and you add our Django app + use the snippet defined in step (3)

Let me know if I'm clear enough. I definitely think we're going to rewrite our Python docs because it's not clear. Also, we'll be sure to make better the `ddtrace-run` script so that it will be the only clear way to instrument all your app, unless advanced usage is required.

Regarding redistributor consumption of these kinds of files:

- distros don't just ship libraries and frameworks, we ship applications and services as well, so these kinds of files may be used directly when creating layered Linux application containers directly from upstream repositories and/or tarballs. In such cases, we need to extract the abstract dependency information in order to automate respinning and retesting those containers for security updates.
- through things like the [Language Server Protocol](https://github.com/Microsoft/language-server-protocol) we aim to make it easier for IDEs to support a wide range of programming languages, and that includes teaching language server implementations to understand the dependency declaration formats used in different ecosystems
- we (and services like libraries.io) also pull deployment centric files from public and private source control repositories in order to analyse and report on real world component *usage*.

While we can (and do) handle language specific dependency declaration formats by analysing them with tools written in those languages, it's much nicer (and typically more reliable) when we don't have to and can instead just use standard file format parsers (with TOML and JSON being preferred to XML and YAML due to the security problems arising with the latter pair in their default parsing configurations).

That said, I *don't* think making our lives easier should be the primary consideration here - as open source consumer facing service providers we pull in the largest share of open source funding, and are hence better equipped than most to adapt to whatever upstream communities decide is in their best interests.

Instead, I think the key point is the one raised by @asottile and others: that regardless of the format chosen *Python developers* are likely to want to write custom tools to help them manage their `Pipfile`, just as they've written tools to help manage `requirements.txt` files.

With TOML, the knowledge of how to perform safe automated file manipulations would be applicable to both `Pipfile` and `pyproject.toml`, while a top-to-bottom imperative format would categorically prohibit a lot of automated transformations, while making others significantly more difficult (and Python version dependent).

This snippet fails when a topic contains only one message. In this case offsets will be like `[(partition, -1)`, and since `-1` is a special offset that is interpreted as latest the offset will not be properly reset.

I think this should fix it:

```
client = KafkaClient(hosts="xxxxxxx")
topic = client.topics['mytopic']
LAST_N_MESSAGES = 1

def consume_latest()
    consumer = topic.get_simple_consumer(
        auto_offset_reset=OffsetType.LATEST,
        reset_offset_on_start=True
    )
    offsets = [(p, op.next_offset - LAST_N_MESSAGES - 1) for p, op in consumer._partitions.iteritems()]
    offsets = [(p, o) if o != -1 else (p, -2) for p, o in offsets]
    consumer.reset_offsets(offsets)
    return consumer.consume()
```

I'm going deep on this today. What I've found so far is this sequence of events:

* `BalancedConsumer` running normally
* In normal operation, a `BalancedConsumer.consume()` call's internal call to `SimpleConsumer.consume()` waits [here](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/6e4698b06571e83f5cecb4a5c94b7514df2c8137/pykafka/simpleconsumer.py#L421) for a new message to arrive. It waits for `_consumer_timeout_ms`.
* While the `SimpleConsumer.consume()` call is blocking, a rebalance is triggered.
* The `consume()` call waits for `_consumer_timeout_ms` and continues. Since a rebalance is in progress, [this line](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/6e4698b06571e83f5cecb4a5c94b7514df2c8137/pykafka/simpleconsumer.py#L433) or the matching line above is reached, causing `ConsumerStoppedException`.
* This causes the `BalancedConsumer` to [immediately re-loop](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/6e4698b06571e83f5cecb4a5c94b7514df2c8137/pykafka/balancedconsumer.py#L732) and [recheck the timeout condition](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/6e4698b06571e83f5cecb4a5c94b7514df2c8137/pykafka/balancedconsumer.py#L725). This condition is `True` since the `consume()` call waited for `_consumer_timeout_ms`.
* At this point, the `BalancedConsumer.consume()` call returns `None`, which is not the correct behavior.

My thought on how to fix this behavior is that there is one basic thing going wrong here: the semaphore condition in `SimpleConsumer.consume()` should not continue to wait when the consumer itself has been stopped. Doing so makes no sense, since `stop()` stops new messages from being fetched. The fix, then, should be to release the semaphore on `stop()` to unblock any so blocked calls to `consume()`.

Applying this fix reveals another issue. Once the `BalancedConsumer` is able to wait on its internal consumer for less than `_consumer_timeout_ms`, the re-loop may still be calling `consume()` on the old `self._consumer`. The rebalancing logic may not have reassigned `self._consumer`. It has [a lot to do](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/6e4698b06571e83f5cecb4a5c94b7514df2c8137/pykafka/balancedconsumer.py#L359-L376) between `stop()`ing the consumer and reassigning `self._consumer`, so this is a common occurrence. Since `self._consumer` [here](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/6e4698b06571e83f5cecb4a5c94b7514df2c8137/pykafka/balancedconsumer.py#L728) is still pointing to the old, stale `SimpleConsumer`, and since the semaphore was only released once by `stop()`, [this logic](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/6e4698b06571e83f5cecb4a5c94b7514df2c8137/pykafka/simpleconsumer.py#L421) blocks once again.

The fix here is to ensure that `self._consumer.consume` cannot be called during a rebalance, so we're always sure that the `self._consumer` we're referencing is the currently valid one.

- the OPAC (public interface for users)

@eustas , it looks like that usual solution is to name static lib differently on Windows, so both .lib files (for shared and static builds) can reside in the same directory.
But it seems that standard naming convention for this is not available. For example, conda-forge's Windows builds add "_static" suffix. Boost adds "lib" prefix for static libs (but it might be because boost's suffixes already depends on build configuration).

Some sample of how "_static" suffix can be added https://github.com/apache/arrow/blob/master/cpp/cmake_modules/BuildUtils.cmake#L151

I'm not sure I know all the answers here, but hopefully @gbernstein can correct me if I'm wrong about any of this.

First, I think we probably wouldn't need `pad_factor`.  Or any of the padding options that we have in the regular InterpolatedImage class.  

I think stepk can probably be taken as the scale of the Fourier image.  I guess we'd want to restrict the image to not have any complicated wcs.  Just a pixel scale, which people should be able to override I guess with a scale parameter to the InterpolatedKImage constructor.

Similarly, maxk will be the size of the image provided * dk.  If we take the provided image as the exact Fourier image, then there is no power at larger k values than this.  We should pad up to the next power of 2 or 3 x power of 2.  But other than this detail, I think we can just take what the user provided.  

Finally, another difference I think is that we'll only have kinterp.  I don't think it makes any sense to consider an xinterp in this case.  Although I guess if we want to allow photon shooting of this type of object, we might need to build the xtab and have some way to interpolate it.  Maybe Gary has some thoughts about what the right interpolant would be to use in this case.

Hi, @alicertel , thanks to you for discussions, If we not talking, we would not have improve this field)

1. You're wrong. All RelatedFields takes pk as data. But SlugRelatedField return slug, PrimaryKeyRelatedField return pk, HyperlinkedRelatedField return hyperlink, look at [this](http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/relations/#hyperlinkedrelatedfield) and etc. SerializableRelatedField return serialized object - not just one entity like string(in case of SlugRelatedField, HyperlinkedRelatedField) or integer(in case of PrimaryKeyRelatedField). Prefix say what we have in representation. It's return a serialized structure. PresentablePrimaryKeyRelatedField doesn't return PresentablePrimaryKey it return serialized structure, so i think, it's a not good name.

2. It's not for representation of object, it's made to valid if related field with this pk exists or not. Look at [source code of PrimaryKeyRelatedField](https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/blob/master/rest_framework/relations.py#L219). It's done analogical, but without `pk_field` checking.

3. I don't think, that it's right place for this idiom, because in use default behaviour that I'm write in docs, and if you pass ModelSerializer in some cases there is no need to pass queryset, because you know model of ModelSerializer, if you want to pass special queryset, you can pass queryset, and if you want with special manager.

4. Extending from PrimaryKeyRelatedField is bad, because it can take `pk_field` argument, that represent object, [look at the docs](http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/relations/#primarykeyrelatedfield), but you don't use it in PresentablePrimaryKeyRelatedField.

I largely agree with Jens, there's no point in dragging along stuff that was added for compatibility with Python 2.4 (since `vsc-base` hasn't actually supported Python 2.4 in a while no), or to spend a lot of time porting stuff that's not actually used (although it's not trivial to be sure that's the case, we have a lot of scripts & other libraries that rely on `vsc-base`, including several private ones).

One step is the right direction is modernizing `FrozenDict`, cfr. #265, which makes it significantly easier to port `frozendict.py` to Python 3.x.

W.r.t. this PR: it has become a bit too unwieldy, I think we should use a step-wise approach towards supporting Python 3.x, e.g.:

* first making sure all `lib/vsc/utils/*.py` modules import without problems in Python 3, by only doing `import` tests rather than actually running the test suite (I already have something fleshed out for that, PR coming up soon)
* once that has been covered, gradually make sure all tests pass on top of Python 3, module by module (via `python setup.py test -F 'foo|bar|baz'`), starting with the modules used by other `vsc.utils.*` modules (e.g. `missing` & `fancylogger`)
* keep PRs small & focused, so they can be reviewed & assessed easily, which will help significantly in getting them merged and actually making progress

Meanwhile, we should:

* be very careful to maintain backward compatibility (or deprecate things properly we don't want to put effort in to port to Python 3), i.e. ensure tests always keep passing using both Python 2.6 & 2.7
* check whether the existing tests actually cover the code being changed, and if not look into adding more tests to increase code coverage

The EasyBuild framework test suite can (also) be used as a smoke test to ensure no functionality is broken in Python 2.x, since it leverages quite a bit of the functionality provided by `vsc-base` (although certainly not everything).

This problem will sometimes happen when the compiler attempts to use advanced instructions on the CPU that are not supported for some reasons. For example, I've seen this happen when DyNet is run in virtualization environments.

One way to fix this is to turn off the use of advanced instructions. This will make things slower (particularly when running on CPU, not GPU), but can probably fix this problem. The way to do so is to open `CMakeLists.txt` in the main DyNet directory, find the string `-march=native`, delete it, then delete your `build` directory and compile DyNet from scratch.

Does this fix the problem? This happens from time-to-time, so we might want to add a compile-time option to make it possible to turn off these advanced instructions.

I kind of like @mjdenkowski's suggestions for adding factors to the source list, and doing the same with num embed. It would not be hard to separate this internally and do a check to ensure the lists were the same length. In generally I like multi-purpose flags instead of new flags if their use cases are clear. But I am also fine to stick with `--source-factors` and `--source-factor-dims`. Michael's thought about later fitting multi-source decoding into this API is a good one. I think this is an argument for keeping `--source-factors` separate — multiple sources would then be multiple arguments to `--source`. So I guess my vote is to keep them separate.

For input, I would *strongly* prefer tab-delimited data to the Moses-style. 
- Tab is already a meta-data character and it is better to make use of that than to introduce new metadata characters that we'd then have to ensure was handled and undone by Sockeye or wrapper scripts. The Moses support mailing list is full of people wondering why their decoder died when it hit a '|'.
- It fits easier with research workflows, where you generate multiple factors separately and store them into files. With the Moses format, you need an intervening script to generate it from the factors. Not a big deal but one of those minor issues that help make life nicer.
- Note that my `paste()` function strips tabs out of each of the input streams before pasting them, so there is no worry about errant tabs in the input. Since tabs should not be there anyway, this function both does not harm and succinctly and easily ensures the data is clean (at least from the tab perspective).

At test time, it seems clean to me to similarly rely only pasted data on STDIN. We should also add support for `--source-factors` there for people passing input via `-s` instead.

Hi @donnemartin @jamesls,

The redraw issues have been solved in this commit: https://github.com/jonathanslenders/python-prompt-toolkit/commit/6be54d6c140bfe2ce5d626de94860daa97172132 (not released yet.)

It was caused by the exception that's raised in ``stop_input_and_refresh_cli`` which was not handled correctly. However, actually, we don't need to raise that exception. We also don't need to quit the ``self.cli.run()`` loop in order to switch between completion types or key bindings.

``KeyBindingManager`` for instance, takes an ``enable_vi_mode`` parameter. This can be a boolean, like it is right now, but it can be an expression that is completely dynamic. In prompt_toolkit, that's what we call a ``Filter``. It should be something like this:

```python
from prompt_toolkit.filters import Condition
...
enable_vi_mode = Condition(lambda cli: get_enable_vi_bindings())
```

For the completions, it's similar. If I find time after the next release, I'll open a pull request with these changes.

Cheers,
Jonathan

> Should we store index entries for the sake of differing HTTP headers over time if the payload/entity body never changes?

Yes!

It is a precious information for non-transactional archives. As long as SURT+datetime (and any other key info kept outside the JSON block) produces unique records, we should keep that. Squashing unchanged entries would be lossy. Here is an example to describe the danger. Suppose a resource was observed every single day for last several years, but the payload never changed till date. We will have thousands of entries in the index, but they all are strong proof that the content didn't change at over observation times over a long period. Now, suppose we squash every entry except the first, now the users would assume we have only observed the resource once, a few years ago, so we have essentially lost the info we gathered. Similarly, preserving the last entry would limit us in expressing when the resource was observed first. If we store first and last entries, then also we will lose the ability to bring confidence that the content was the same in the interim. There are ways to express the confidence during the replay time that the content was not changed in certain windows of time, but that can only be done if the underlying index data is preserved and not squashed. We can even allow a query option for the client to ask for a TimeMap that only lists the URI-Ms with changed content. OWB exposes this information in its tabular view by putting asterisks when a changes were observed.

The issue is that the encoding depends on what features were negotiated between the peers, so a single binary blob cam sometimes be understood differently.

This is why ExaBGP let you specify what you expect to be the setup with a neighbor and then decode the data.

You can use command such as exabgp `config-file` --decode `HEX_MESSAGE` to decode a packet.
Taking an example from issue 381:

```
./sbin/exabgp qa/conf/flow.conf -d --decode "ff ff ff ff ff ff ff ff ff ff ff ff ff ff ff ff 00 32 02 00 00 00 1b 80 0e 09 00 01 85 00 00 03 0c 80 0c 40 01 01 02 c0 10 08 80 06 00 00 00 00 00 00"
```

The `DWaveSampler` uses the D-Wave Quantum Processing Unit (QPU) as a computational resource. Quantum annealing processors naturally return low-energy solutions, but they are inherently probabilistic.

For example, you can imagine sampling from a single qubit with no bias and no interactions with other qubits:
```
>>> sampler = DWaveSampler()
>>> resp = sampler.sample_ising({0: 0.0}, {}, num_reads=1)
```
Everything else being equal, the probability of the qubit ending in the -1 or the 1 state is equal (50 percent).

In another example, say we strongly bias a single qubit towards -1:
```
>>> sampler = DWaveSampler()
>>> resp = sampler.sample_ising({0: 2.0}, {})
```
Even in this case, there is a small chance that the QPU will end the anneal in an excited state and return 1, rather than -1.

It is also important to note that because this sampling is done via a physical process, there is no 'state' to fix.

I hope this helps!

Alex

There are a few options here, given in order of preference:

1. Make your test have another timestep.  Presumably when you're actually training your model, you'll have more timesteps?  Otherwise why do you have those parameters in the first place?
2. Move the gradient check out of the `ensure_model_can_train_save_and_load()` test, and make each model call that test individually.  Your test can just not call that method, because it's not checking anything useful for you.  It's a little annoying though, because it'll need more setup if it's not inside the existing method.
3. After doing 2, make the method configurable, so that only _some_ of the parameters are checked.  Then you can still have the check for `input2hidden` but not `hidden2hidden`.

If we think it'll be a not-too-uncommon occurrence that you will have models that only sometimes have gradients (e.g., multi-task training, GANs, other kinds of multi-stage setups), then we should at least do 2, so that the gradient issue doesn't cause the main test to fail.  Though, you can probably fix those failures in the multi-task setting by making sure that the dataset has all of the tasks...  In any event, my guess is that 1 will be enough for you right now, unless I'm misunderstanding your issue.

Hey sorry for not getting back to this post-mortem, but thank you for posting this after our debugging session!

From our conversation, there was a multitude of problems, but all of it stemmed from ceph falling flat on its face. It's actually well-known in the ceph community that reaching the threshold of disk usage in certain nodes can cause some pretty fantastic errors.

> I install SNMP on all of my nodes to keep an eye on disk usage, and this node was filling up its disk and had reached about 84% of 80GB.

This node in particular likely caused the issue. The resolution usually is to re-balance OSDs so that the data is nicely distributed across the hosts, but that requires manual intervention *before* the cluster starts to fail. For that reason that's why most users go with S3 for a maintenance-free storage backend.

Because of ceph failing over, your database was not able to restore from ceph, which caused database/platform downtime.

> So, this issue is for postmortem and LTS supporting purposes. If there was a safer or smarter way to regenerate my database (deisctl stop database && deisctl uninstall database then deisctl install database && deisctl start database), or if there was some way for me to avoid or detect my WAL archiving failures so I could avoid destroying the database (I think it was still working), or or or...

In most cases, it is as easy as you describe. `deisctl stop database && deisctl uninstall database && deisctl install database && deisctl start database`) will regenerate the database. In v2, kubernetes has healthchecks so it will automatically restart the database if it goes into an error state such as with WAL archiving failures. Fleet has no such feature for us to utilize.

> I would be asking for full-stack health monitoring of Deis platform services.

While this isn't something we're planning on implementing in the v1 line, this is something we addressed in v2 as there is a monitoring stack built into the platform via grafana/influxdb/fluentd/telegraf. See https://github.com/deis/monitor for more information.

In fact, v1 has been in LTS mode for over a year, so we're planning on end-of-life'ing it relatively soon. If you're still on v1 we strongly encourage you to try v2.8.0.

Hope this addresses your issue. Please let me know if I missed something! :)

Here is my naive fix

```
def sign_message_hash(key, msg_hash):
    signature = key.sign_msg_hash(msg_hash)
    (v_standard, r, s) = signature.vrs
    v = v_standard + V_OFFSET

    # Pad it like Solidity expects for bytes32
    r_b = to_bytes(r)
    s_b = to_bytes(s)
    r_b = pad_left(r_b, 32, b"\0")
    s_b = pad_left(s_b, 32, b"\0")

    eth_signature_bytes = b''.join(map(to_bytes, (r_b, s_b, v)))

    return (v, r_b, s_b, eth_signature_bytes)

```

I think I've found the problem and solution. My reproduction was different though.
Django signals are used to observe every new connection and call psycopg2's register_hstore function on it.
Django signals will, by default, store references to the receiver function as weakrefs.
In a low memory situation these can go away and future connections are not registered to use hstore features.
My suggested fix is on PR-128 - https://github.com/djangonauts/django-hstore/pull/128

Another way this problem manifests itself is when sitting in a source folder, python will find appropriately named folders with ``__init__.py`` files in them, and happily use them instead of vendored dependencies. In general, if you're executing code in a folder that has python files in it (not just data or whatever) you might have a bad time.  Everyone is advised to create packages, and perhaps install them in development mode as necessary.

Just for the record, I had a similar issue while building a package for `service_identity`.

Even though the requirement `pyasn1-modules` was buildable because conda have `pyasn1` up to `0.1.9`, it turns out that when building `service_identity` the "requirement chain" resulted in installing `pyasn1 0.1.7`, which I believe it's due to either `cryptography` or `pyopenssl` (I haven't dig further into this), causing the build to fail with the `Setuptools downloading is disabled ...` because `pyasn1-modules` had as requirement `pyasn1 >= 0.1.8`.

So, the simple solution was to build `pyasn1-modules 0.0.5` rather than the latest version.

This is telling you that there were no indices matching your patterns, resulting in an empty list, so nothing was deleted.

It sounds more like you have no indices, or something in your pattern matching is wrong.  What do you see if you enable `loglevel: DEBUG` in the `logging` block of the client yaml file?  You'll see all of the indices being evaluated with this set, which will be helpful for troubleshooting.

I appreciate the suggestion, but to me it makes no sense to add a _Watch_ index to a _Kibana_-specific filter.  In any case, preventing any index starting in `.watch` from being acted on is as simple as adding another filter block:

```
- filtertype: pattern
 kind: prefix
 value: .watch
 exclude: True
```

It wouldn't be too hard to add another filtertype for watches, but that seems a lot of work to replicate the above functionality for a _single_ index, or index pattern. The Kibana filtertype exists because it existed in previous versions (and as such, it would be expected), and there are multiple, dissimilar indices which are used by Kibana/Marvel, so this prevents the user from having to add multiple filter blocks to catch all of those.

If you don't install `certifi` using SSL will fail, if you don't provide any CA data yourself. 

`certifi` isn't installed by default. It's an optional dependency. 

If your ES cluster is using self-signed certs certifi won't help you because it doesn't have any CA data for self signed certs. 
If your ES cluster is using pubicly create (lets-encrypt or some other "for-pay" signing service) then Certifi will work, but you have to install that dependency yourself.

```python
In [1]: from autograd import jacobian_vector_product

In [2]: import autograd.numpy as np

   ...:     return x**2 + 1
   ...:

In [4]: jvp = jacobian_vector_product(f)

In [6]: v = np.array([4., 5., 6.])

In [7]: jvp(x, v)
Out[7]: array([  8.,  20.,  36.])
```

Hi @galgalesh,

I worked on the bigtop charms that use puppet with @johnsca, @kwmonroe, @petevg and @andrewdmcleod . They might have more concrete ideas how a concrete integration would look like.

Overall Juju+Puppet are a good match. I think we were seeing two main patterns on how puppet was used:
- In the first pattern puppet was doing the initial setup and then juju was taking over to shape the deployment based on the relations available and the config changes. I think this was more of a necessity rather than a design choice. Probably because the puppet scripts were not as flexible as we wanted them to be so we had to "tune" the deployment we got from puppet.
- In the second pattern juju was collecting all the env parameters and was feeding them to puppet. Puppet would re-configuring/re-deploy the system based on the environment presented to it. In this pattern we had to make sure that all the juju configs and relations could be mapped to puppet config params. Spark should be closer to this pattern.

The first pattern is for those who are more comfortable with juju the second for those who have a mature puppet deployment story.

I do not think I have a good answer to your question, this is just my take on puppet + juju.

@omaciel, warnings are a special type of exception. Check out the [`exception hierarchy`](https://docs.python.org/3.4/library/exceptions.html#exception-hierarchy). As you can see, `Warning` is a sub-class of `Exception`.

I say that warnings are a "special" type of exception because they are handled differently from a normal exception. When a normal exception is raised and goes uncaught, the currently executing statement stops. When a warning exception is raised and goes uncaught, the currently executing statement completes and a message is printed to stderr.

Python allows you to hook in to the warnings system in two ways. First, you can choose to emit warnings in ways other than just printing them to stderr. You can do this with "handlers". For example, I could install a second handler that fires off an email whenever a warning is emitted. In this case, an un-caught warning would cause two things to happen:

* A message is printed to stderr.
* An email is sent.

Handlers can be arbitrarily complex. You could even install a handler that causes your browser to open a new tab and show a [sad panda](https://i.imgur.com/n67E6wm.png) each time an ERROR level error is emitted.

OK, so the first way to hook in to the warnings system is installing new handlers. The second way you can hook in to the warnings system is to filter out warnings and prevent them from being emitted at all. That's what I'm doing here.

Unfortunately, the warnings system is process-wide. This is reflected in the logic of the various methods that the warnings module exposes. For example, you can do this to temporarily filter all warnings:

```python
with warnings.catch_warnings():
    …
```

The above is not as great as it seems, though. What this does is make a copy of the current set of warning filters, installs a new filter that catches all warnings, runs the nested code, and then restores the original set of warning filters. This only behaves well in a single-threaded context. Lets's say that you have a Python application that is using two threads, and the first thread raises a CRITICAL level warning message. Let's also say that this happens while the second thread is using `warnings.catch_warnings`. In this case, the first thread's warning is filtered out by the new filter installed by the second thread, and you never get to see the critical warning. Uh oh. :(

That's a huge weakness of the logic in this pull request. All warnings of type `requests.packages.urllib3.exceptions.InsecureRequestWarning` raised absolutely anywhere in your entire application are now silenced. The reason I haven't submitted this pull request earlier is because of that major flaw. However, I think that training users to ignore warnings is a bigger risk, so I think that this change is worth it.

You're not allowed to use the consumer again after you call close() on it,
I suggest removing the close() call, possibly replacing it with unsubscribe() (which is optional),
or creating a new consumer.


Also, creating a producer for each message will be very slow due to the startup costs of the client (initiating connections, acquiring metadata, etc), it is better to reuse an existing producer throughout your application.

This is somewhat unfair in a few ways. For one thing, this is not an incremental change in the sense that it is not a change that moves us towards a situation we want to be in. Something like this will happen as part of the "making `_timelex` public", but it won't be this specifically, so this PR would just get undone. The job of the PathMapper class is to translate the host paths (which would be Windows path, or better file:/// URIs) to the correct paths inside the container.

Here's a diff/patch (on the function stageFiles() in the file process.py) I used to deal with this issue: 
--- process.py.orig     2016-07-26 08:55:35.000000000 -0500
+++ process.py  2016-10-12 10:30:42.357323872 -0500
@@ -172,8 +172,9 @@
 def stageFiles(pm, stageFunc, ignoreWritable=False):
     # type: (PathMapper, Callable[..., Any]) -> None
     for f, p in pm.items():
-        if not os.path.exists(os.path.dirname(p.target)):
-            os.makedirs(os.path.dirname(p.target), 0755)
+        dirname = os.path.dirname(p.target)
+        if dirname and not os.path.exists(dirname):
+            os.makedirs(dirname, 0755)
         if p.type == "File":
             stageFunc(p.resolved, p.target)
         elif p.type == "WritableFile" and not ignoreWritable:

The diff here is showing only 1 changed line. You could be seeing more changes locally, but it depends on how you've configured your git client. In general, the best git procedure for pvlib users is to:

1. fork the library
2. clone your fork to your computer
2. make a branch dedicated to the small change that you want to make
2. checkout the new branch
2. make the changes, commit the changes
2. push the new branch to your github fork
2. make a pull request from your fork's branch

The key ideas are to keep your local master up to date with ``pvlib/master`` (use git fetch and git rebase or git pull) and do not make any changes to your local master. Only make changes in branches, with one branch for each pull request. Note that some git clients will automatically refer to this as ``upstream/master``.

Ok, so the three things we need are:
- `localDir` - the full(?) path to the directory holding the local file (source for a `put` and target for a `get`)
- `workerSubDir` - the subdirectory under the working dir on the WN that the file is copied *to* when it's an input file and copied *from* when it's an output file
- `remoteDir` (or `storageDir`) - the full(?) path to the directory (e.g. in the DFC or on EOS) where the file is copied *from* if it's an input file (i.e. `DiracFile` data) and copied *to* if it's an output file w.r.t. the WN. For local access to the file, it's used as the target for a `put` and the source for a `get`.

If someone wants to chain up an output file as an input file then this will work fine as `remoteDir` will be consistent in the two use-cases and is independent of where the file gets copied to on the WN (`workerSubDir`).

These three attributes work as explained above for a `DiracFile`. For a `LocalFile`, `remoteDir` would not make sense (or at least is logically the same as the `localDir`). `LocalFile`s are different to the other files types anyway as the WN code is less responsible for dealing with them.

@balloob sure, I see it in get_entries. My concern is that this implementation in such way drops all other vendors supporting lpd via Bonjour. I'd rather would like to see it as LPD printer discovery allowing any vendors. I think filtering by vendor name should be done in corresponding component setup which uses some vendor specifics. Actual filter for "HP " of service name does not looks like "standardized" way to define that vendor is HP (if I understood correctly what `find_by_device_name ` method does). End user is able to define Bonjour name of she's device in device settings (i.e remove call it w/o vendor prefix for example).  Filtering to detect vendor has to use TXT record fields and this process is actually vendor specific and error prone - the best is to try USB_MFG field but i'm pretty sure it is absent for LDP service type you use, you can try `ty` and/or `product` which again not standardized even in single vendor. So again  - imo it is a reason to avoid such filtering at all to cover as much devices as possible and allow end user to see them in hass :) Another concern is that if basically the target to find a device capable to print then probably you need to discover all of service types described by bonjourprinting spec and put service type into discovery callback.

Proposed solution for a 32 columns-wide font:
```python
>>> solution = '{:<24}{:>8}\n'.format('left', 'right')
'left                    abcdabcd'
>>> p.text(solution)
```
Where 24 is the maximum character count of the left part and 8 is the maximum character count of the right part (24+8 = 32 columns). See [Python .format() examples](https://docs.python.org/2/library/string.html#format-examples).

We should draft something better than this hack for software tabs I think, but use this trick for implementation.

```Bash
invoke tag --name=foo --message="This is a test"
```

There is no tie-in between the ``tag`` task and the ``pypi`` task.  That way a developer can create a Git tag however they wish prior to using the ``pypi`` task to publish to PyPI.

So the cerbot roadmap lives in github milestones - the two upcoming ones are 0.13.0 and 0.14.0 which can be viewed here: https://github.com/certbot/certbot/milestone/34 and https://github.com/certbot/certbot/milestone/35
We have a more general milestone for 1.0 where we've been making sure to place any issues that we think are needed for our first non-beta release: https://github.com/certbot/certbot/milestone/8
I'd think that it's important that we make sure any discrepancies with the acme spec are documented and at the very least end up in that milestone - so feel free to open specific issues about places where we don't meet the spec and I'll make sure that they get tracked.
The certbot team additionally has weekly meetings where we review our progress on the current milestone and do things like move things out of or into it from larger milestones like 1.0
If you want to chat with us about what we're prioritizing, or specific features that you think we're not tracking #letsencrypt-dev on irc.freenode.net is where we mostly hang out and talk about this stuff.
If you have an interest in submitting code to certbot you'd be welcome to join the weekly meetings and talk about your progress in getting us in line with the spec.
Alternately I think a number of us would be interested in having a conversation about how to implement some of the features we don't support yet - so if you have thoughts on implementation or arcitecture I'd be happy to schedule separate specific meetings.

```
    books = models.ForeignKey(

        default=None,
        on_delete=SET_NULL,
```

One bug I encountered while testing this, which is not an issue with this PR and should be fixed separately (but is interesting nonetheless), is the following.

This is mostly an issue with the fact that functions are currently identified by their module + name, so functions with the same name override each other.

```python
import ray
import numpy as np

ray.init()

@ray.remote
def f(n):
  return np.zeros(n)

# Some of these objects are evicted.
l = [f.remote(100000000) for _ in range(1000)]

@ray.remote
def f(x, y):
  return x + y

# Do a tree reduction.
while len(l) > 1:
  l.append(f.remote(l.pop(0), l.pop(0)))

ray.get(l)
```

When some of the original tasks that created the list `l` are re-executed, the new definition of the function `f` will be used, and you will get an error like this

```
Remote function __main__.f failed with:

Traceback (most recent call last):
TypeError: f() missing 1 required positional argument: 'y'
```
I cannot make the template operator function virtual and the return type is `RayLogBase`, which could be the cause. I will make a fix soon.

@yuyiming and @imzhenyu) thanks for the comments. I added another cleanup proposal which should work for arbitrarily interleaved batches. See "Garbage collection for interleaving batches" here: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1sMgVMajomF5j-GDkzhuejrAwLcxXNKY9lTmlDo7GkqM/edit?ts=5b360317#.

In a nutshell here is the scheme: 
1. Create a dependency graph across batches.
2. Identify connected components in this graph.
3. When a batch finishes checkpoint all its return values, and the state of any external actor it has modified.
4. When all batches in a connected component have been checkpointed, clean up the state of all the batches in the component.

I suggest that once we are comfortable with this or another scheme, we are planning the implementation.

I haven't looked at uvloop in detail, but I did look at libuv fairly carefully, and AFAICT libuv is missing some features that trio needs. The main one is that most libuv operations don't support cancellation, but there are also some minor semantic mismatches like, iirc they don't have any way to call `accept` *once*, just a way to say "start `accept`ing on this socket". (Maybe you can immediately tell it to stop from the first `accept` callback?) These issues reflect mismatches between the classic reactor API style like node and asyncio use, but trio doesn't. It's quite possible these can be handled (e.g., by submitting patches to libuv to support cancellation on more operations), but it would take some time and attention to work out.

Uvloop in particular also has the problem that because it's implemented as a C extension, it's probably considerably slower on pypy than CPython (if it works at all). The tradeoffs here are complicated and application specific, but certainly for many projects pypy will be more of a win than uvloop, if they have to choose. A cffi-based wrapper for libuv is probably a better starting point? (If someone wants to experiment then [this actually exists](https://uv.readthedocs.io/) Also iiuc a lot of the magic in uvloop is careful optimization of asyncio-specific code, like a C implementation of their `Future` class, which wouldn't automatically carry over to trio anyway.

It's also likely that the low hanging fruit for optimizations is currently inside trio, not the actual io code. E.g. trio's decision to insist that all io primitives are checkpoints will definitely hurt it in throughput microbenchmarks; it's a trade-off I made intentionally to optimize for latency and correctness (it helps avoid cases where one client starves out there others). But it's also a place where the current implementation is stupid-simple and I know we can optimize away a lot of the added overhead with some cleverness :-). But that would need to be done in trio itself.

Have you seen the discussion of optimization in the [design docs](http://trio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/design.html#high-level-design-principles)? An underlying problem here is that microbenchmarks like in the uvloop blog post can be super impressive, but are often a terrible guide to making anything better in the real world; they encourage a focus on trivialities and make it harder to make appropriate trade-offs. (Note that this isn't a criticism of uvloop in general – I'm pretty sure it was developed using real applications, and you have to communicate things somehow. And microbenchmarks definitely have a useful role to play. It's when winning the microbenchmarks becomes your goal that I think this really harmful to good engineering.) So ultimately I don't think there's much that *can* be said about trio's performance until we have some real apps to look at.

In the mean time do check out pypy if you're worried about performance, you might be surprised ;-)

So, I did a little more digging and you're right. The `django-markdown` codebase does push those attributes through to the widget, irrespective of their values. The snag lies in Django (we're using 1.11) in one of the widget template files: 
```
# templates/django/forms/widgets/attrs.html

{% for name, value in widget.attrs.items %}{% if value is not False %} {{ name }}{% if value is not True %}="{{ value }}"{% endif %}{% endif %}{% endfor %}
```
Note that Django will completely omit an attribute if its value is False. So, I suppose the alternative to doing 'hasAttribute()' checks would be to coerce the `MARKDOWNX_EDITOR_RESIZABLE` variable to string before sending through to Django's Textarea widget.

Having said that, the hasAttribute() check already gets called in the intialization function so it only gets called once per editor element (not on every update). So, performance-wise it should be fine?

I got no luck with jinja2. The closest choice was [ChoiceLoader](https://github.com/pallets/jinja/blob/master/jinja2/loaders.py#L376) but it is clever enough to understand `setup.py.jj2` extends another `setup.py.jj2`, which locates in a different but sequential ordered folder.

The work around would be putting files under a special directory:

.moban.dt/local-override/setup.py.jj2

And then use this configuration{

```
configuration:
  configuration_dir: "commons/config"
  template_dir:
    - ".moban.dt"
    - "commons/templates"
    - "setupmobans/templates"
  configuration: your.yml
targets:
  - setup.py: local-override/setup.py.jj2
```

Well, technically, there could be one more choice is to implement such a overlapping template loader by extending jinja2's baseloader. However, this may take some unknown time.

With your suggested parameters it takes **2.1s**.

I tried to vary every option (separately) and was able to bring it down to **1.2s** with:
 * `M = 2`

The sequence that worked for me (based on @ramkv<https://github.com/ramkv> post) was:

  1.  Uninstall JPype1 and remove all possible left over folders under lib/site-packages.
  2.  In a command window set JAVA_HOME as set JAVA_HOME=C:\Progra~2\Java\jdk1.8.0_181 (do not enclose 8.3 path into double quotes).
  3.  Install Jpype1 with --no-cache-dir option (pip --no-cache-dir install jpype1).

After that the test mini-program started working. Before that it was crashing python.exe with access violation at the same point as in your case.

I tend to believe that there is an issue with JPype1 setup/linking step when JAVA_HOME is not set or set to a path with spaces in it.

As soon as correctly installed/linked on my Win 7 JPype works with both JRE 7 and JRE 8 and I can use regular windows paths with spaces to jvm.dll
